title: Turkish delight
subtitle: Rosewater-scented cornflour gel cut into cubes and dusted with icing sugar
excerpt: The authentic method for Turkish delight (lokum) uses cornflour cooked into a hot sugar syrup for 30 to 45 minutes until very thick and translucent, flavoured with rosewater and tinted pink, then set overnight before cutting and dusting. No gelatine. The texture is soft and yielding but holds its shape without collapsing. Requires patience: the cornflour cook is long and requires constant attention.
sourceNotes: Lokum (Turkish delight) has been made in Turkey since at least the 15th century, though the modern cornflour-set version is attributed to Haci Bekir, a confectioner who opened a shop in Istanbul in 1777. The starch-based method (cooking cornflour into a sugar syrup) predates the modern gelatine shortcut and is used in Turkish commercial and home production. The sweet was introduced to Britain in the 19th century through Ottoman trade connections and became widely known through the popular British confectionery market, including through the Fry's Turkish Delight bar introduced in 1914.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Turkish delight must be made at least 8 hours ahead (overnight is ideal) to set fully. It keeps for 2 weeks in an airtight container at room temperature, re-dusted with icing sugar if needed.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: target sugar temperature (thread stage). The sugar syrup is cooked to 107°C before the cornflour slurry is added. A sugar thermometer is recommended. Do not exceed 115°C or the final texture will be too firm.

recipeTools.notes: two saucepans needed: one for the sugar syrup, one for the cornflour slurry

glossaryTerms.definition: The setting mechanism of authentic Turkish delight: cornflour (starch) cooked in a hot sugar syrup until it gelatinises and forms a thick, translucent gel. The starch granules absorb water and swell, creating a network that holds the sugar in a solid mass. The long cooking time (30 to 45 minutes of constant stirring) is needed to fully gelatinise the starch and cook off enough water for the gel to set firm. Unlike gelatine, starch gel does not melt at body temperature, giving lokum its characteristic bite-and-yield texture.

content.text: , not gelatine. The cooking time is long and requires constant stirring. Use a silicone spatula that reaches into the corners of the pan; the starch will catch and burn if ignored. The finished mixture should be thick enough that a channel drawn through it with the spatula closes back slowly. Set overnight before cutting.

attrs.prepNote: pink or red gel food colouring

content.text: Oil a 20 cm square tin lightly. Dissolve the sugar in 300 ml water with the lemon juice in a saucepan over low heat. Increase the heat and cook to 107°C on a sugar thermometer. Keep warm on very low heat. In a second saucepan, whisk the 100 g cornflour into the remaining 300 ml cold water until smooth. Place over medium heat and cook, stirring constantly, until the mixture thickens to a stiff paste.

Gradually pour the hot sugar syrup into the cornflour paste, whisking constantly to prevent lumps. Once combined, cook over medium-low heat, stirring continuously, for 30 to 45 minutes until the mixture becomes very thick, turns slightly translucent, and pulls away cleanly from the sides of the pan. Stir in the rosewater and food colouring. Pour into the oiled tin and smooth the surface. Leave to cool completely, then refrigerate overnight.

Mix the icing sugar and 25 g cornflour together. Dust a board generously with the mixture. Turn out the set Turkish delight and cut into 3 cm squares with an oiled knife. Toss each piece in the dusting mixture to coat. Store in an airtight container, separating layers with extra dusting mix.
